-{"id":"ft-2mvmx","title":"Watcher clients + per-client view-state model for agent swarms","description":"## Goal\nAdd explicit watcher-client semantics (read-only observers) and per-client view state (active tab/pane/mirror mode) to support multi-agent sessions safely.\n\n## Why\nZellij’s watcher + per-client state model maps directly to FrankenTerm multi-agent orchestration and reduces contention/ambiguity around shared focus.\n\n## Scope\n- Define client roles: interactive vs watcher (read-only).\n- Track per-client view state (active tab/pane, mirrored or independent view).\n- Enforce role-based policy for mutating actions (, pane kill, workflow mutation).\n- Surface role/view information in  and session APIs.\n\n## Deliverables\n- Session/client model update + policy hooks + API exposure.\n- Tests for role enforcement and multi-client focus semantics.\n\n## Acceptance criteria\n- Watchers cannot perform mutating actions.\n- Interactive clients preserve current behavior.\n- State visibility is deterministic for orchestration tools.\n\n## Cross-references\n- Zellij session analysis: \n- Session persistence epic: \n- /dp integration epic: ","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-12T06:42:57.379276Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-12T06:42:57.379276Z"}
-{"id":"ft-3rwhd","title":"Cross-subsystem action completion tokens + cause-chain context","description":"## Goal\nImplement a logical completion protocol for multi-step operations spanning capture/storage/policy/workflow/event bus, with end-to-end cause-chain context.\n\n## Why\nPrevents racey partial completion and improves debuggability under load; mirrors Zellij route-thread completion-token pattern.\n\n## Scope\n- Add completion token object carried across subsystem instructions.\n- Define logical completion boundaries for key operations (, workflow-triggered actions, recovery actions).\n- Propagate cause-chain context through internal channels and audit records.\n- Add timeout + failure semantics with explicit terminal states.\n\n## Deliverables\n- Runtime contract doc + implementation for completion tokens.\n- Tests covering success, timeout, and partial-failure paths.\n\n## Acceptance criteria\n- No operation reports success before logical completion boundary.\n- Timeout/failure paths preserve enough context for postmortem.\n- No deadlock/regression in existing workflow execution paths.\n\n## Cross-references\n- Zellij inventory analysis: \n- Fork hardening epic: \n- asupersync epic: ","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-12T06:42:57.369688Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-12T06:42:57.369688Z"}
+{"id":"ft-2mvmx","title":"Watcher clients + per-client view-state model for agent swarms","description":"## Goal\nAdd explicit watcher-client semantics (read-only observers) and per-client view state (active tab/pane/mirror mode) to support multi-agent sessions safely.\n\n## Why\nZellij’s watcher + per-client state model maps directly to FrankenTerm multi-agent orchestration and reduces contention/ambiguity around shared focus.\n\n## Scope\n- Define client roles: interactive vs watcher (read-only).\n- Track per-client view state (active tab/pane, mirrored or independent view).\n- Enforce role-based policy for mutating actions (, pane kill, workflow mutation).\n- Surface role/view information in  and session APIs.\n\n## Deliverables\n- Session/client model update + policy hooks + API exposure.\n- Tests for role enforcement and multi-client focus semantics.\n\n## Acceptance criteria\n- Watchers cannot perform mutating actions.\n- Interactive clients preserve current behavior.\n- State visibility is deterministic for orchestration tools.\n\n## Cross-references\n- Zellij session analysis: \n- Session persistence epic: \n- /dp integration epic: ","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-12T06:42:57.379276Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-12T06:44:24.185984Z","closed_at":"2026-02-12T06:44:24.166081Z","close_reason":"Superseded by wa-3jewu (same intent, corrected prefix/description)."}
+{"id":"ft-3rwhd","title":"Cross-subsystem action completion tokens + cause-chain context","description":"## Goal\nImplement a logical completion protocol for multi-step operations spanning capture/storage/policy/workflow/event bus, with end-to-end cause-chain context.\n\n## Why\nPrevents racey partial completion and improves debuggability under load; mirrors Zellij route-thread completion-token pattern.\n\n## Scope\n- Add completion token object carried across subsystem instructions.\n- Define logical completion boundaries for key operations (, workflow-triggered actions, recovery actions).\n- Propagate cause-chain context through internal channels and audit records.\n- Add timeout + failure semantics with explicit terminal states.\n\n## Deliverables\n- Runtime contract doc + implementation for completion tokens.\n- Tests covering success, timeout, and partial-failure paths.\n\n## Acceptance criteria\n- No operation reports success before logical completion boundary.\n- Timeout/failure paths preserve enough context for postmortem.\n- No deadlock/regression in existing workflow execution paths.\n\n## Cross-references\n- Zellij inventory analysis: \n- Fork hardening epic: \n- asupersync epic: ","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-12T06:42:57.369688Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-12T06:44:24.171386Z","closed_at":"2026-02-12T06:44:24.171372Z","close_reason":"Superseded by wa-33uf8 (same intent, corrected prefix/description)."}
 {"id":"ft-oegrb","title":"[EPIC] Universal mux I/O flight recorder + hybrid lexical/semantic search","description":"# [EPIC] Universal Mux I/O Flight Recorder + Hybrid Search (FrankenSQLite + Tantivy + Semantic)

## Vision
Build a **flight recorder** for FrankenTerm that captures **all text entering and leaving the mux server** in an ultra-performant append-only pipeline, then exposes fast, trustworthy, hybrid lexical/semantic retrieval for operators and agents.

This epic is intentionally broader than simple logging. It defines a full system:
1. Capture/tap points for ingress and egress text
2. Durable append-only write path under high concurrency
3. Indexing and retrieval architecture (lexical + semantic)
4. Query/API surfaces for humans and robots
5. Reliability, observability, and rollout safety

## Why this matters
- Multi-agent terminal swarms generate high-volume, high-value forensic data.
- Current observability is good for event/state workflows, but not yet a true end-to-end immutable transcript plane optimized for forensic replay and hybrid retrieval.
- A robust flight recorder increases debugging speed, incident response quality, postmortem accuracy, and model/operator feedback loops.

## Design intent
- **Performance first**: append-only, batched writes, minimal per-event overhead.
- **Correctness first**: total ordering/causality metadata sufficient for replay and audit.
- **Durability first**: resilient write path (including exploration of FrankenSQLite multi-writer and WAL self-healing capabilities).
- **Search first**: Tantivy-backed lexical retrieval plus semantic retrieval and rank fusion.
- **Operator first**: stable CLI/robot/MCP interfaces and runbooks.

## Research grounding
Implementation must be informed by close study/adaptation patterns from:
- `/dp/frankensqlite`
- `/dp/coding_agent_session_search` (cass)
- `/dp/xf`

## Non-goals (for this epic)
- Replacing all existing storage paths in one unsafe big-bang migration.
- Shipping semantic retrieval without lexical fallback and measurable quality baselines.
- Adding unbounded retention without explicit cost/retention controls.

## Deliverables
- Full bead graph (research -> architecture -> implementation -> validation -> rollout)
- Feature-gated implementation plan with rollback path
- Search quality/perf test strategy and success thresholds
- Operator docs and migration guidance

## Success criteria
- Flight recorder captures ingress+egress text with stable ordering metadata.
- Append-only writer sustains swarm-scale load without destabilizing watcher/runtime.
- Lexical and hybrid search return correct results with measurable latency/quality targets.
- End-to-end system is testable, observable, and safe to roll out incrementally.

## Acceptance criteria
- All child tracks are completed or explicitly deferred with rationale.
- Dependency graph is acyclic and implementation-ready for swarm execution.
- Every major component includes unit/integration/e2e/load tests and failure-mode coverage.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-12T06:02:26.151022Z","created_by":"WildSpring","updated_at":"2026-02-12T06:02:26.151022Z","labels":["epic","flight-recorder","frankensqlite","search","semantic","tantivy"]}
 {"id":"ft-oegrb.1","title":"Research track: deep source-study + adaptation plan for frankensqlite/cass/xf","description":"## Track objective
Create a rigorous, implementation-grade understanding of `/dp/frankensqlite`, `/dp/coding_agent_session_search` (cass), and `/dp/xf`, then translate that into directly actionable adaptation guidance for FrankenTerm.

## Why this track exists
Premature implementation without deep source-level study risks cargo-culting and architecture drift. This track produces the factual foundation for all subsequent tracks.

## Deliverables
- Deep-dive dossiers per source project
- Cross-project architecture extraction matrix
- Adapt-vs-rewrite decisions with risk/cost rationale
- Final ADR describing the target flight-recorder/search architecture

## Acceptance criteria
- No downstream implementation bead should require re-reading external repos to understand scope.
- All key assumptions and tradeoffs are documented with explicit reasoning and test implications.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-12T06:02:58.090904Z","created_by":"WildSpring","updated_at":"2026-02-12T06:02:58.090904Z","labels":["cass","flight-recorder","frankensqlite","research","xf"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ft-oegrb.1","depends_on_id":"ft-oegrb","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-12T06:02:58.090904Z","created_by":"WildSpring","metadata":"{}"}]}
 {"id":"ft-oegrb.1.1","title":"Study /dp/frankensqlite deeply for append-only recorder suitability","description":"## Goal
Perform a code-level architectural study of `/dp/frankensqlite` focused on applicability to flight-recorder ingestion at swarm scale.

## Background
We need evidence-based decisions about adopting FrankenSQLite capabilities (multi-writer concurrency, WAL durability model, self-healing behaviors, operational characteristics) instead of speculative integration.

## Scope
- Map write path internals relevant to append-only transcript ingest.
- Identify integration seams (API boundaries, transaction model, backpressure behavior, checkpointing).
- Characterize durability and recovery semantics, including WAL fault scenarios.
- Document required adapter layer in FrankenTerm.

## Deliverables
- Self-contained technical dossier with architecture diagrams, hot-path notes, risks, and recommended integration patterns.
- "Adopt as-is vs adapt vs avoid" table for key FrankenSQLite subsystems.

## Acceptance criteria
- Dossier includes concrete code references and explicit assumptions.
- At least one proposed integration path and one fallback path are described.
- Open risks are converted into explicit downstream beads.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-12T06:03:44.499014Z","created_by":"WildSpring","updated_at":"2026-02-12T06:11:06.125639Z","labels":["flight-recorder","frankensqlite","research"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ft-oegrb.1.1","depends_on_id":"ft-oegrb.1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-12T06:03:44.499014Z","created_by":"WildSpring","metadata":"{}"}]}

 {"id":"wa-1ta8j","title":"Proactive pane lifecycle management — zombie reaping, stuck detection, age-based cleanup","description":"# Proactive Pane Lifecycle Management

## Skills: /extreme-software-optimization, /idea-wizard

## Problem
At 200+ panes, abandoned/stuck agents accumulate silently. A 54-hour agent consuming 118GB RAM was observed in production. Zombie processes consume process table slots. Stuck test processes (12+ hours) waste CPU.

## Solution: Activity-Based Lifecycle Engine

**NOTE**: This bead CONSUMES process_triage (wa-2zywl) as a library — it does NOT reimplement zombie reaping or stuck detection. process_triage provides the ProcessClassifier/TriageEngine/WhackAMoleDetector; this bead adds the FrankenTerm-specific health classification and pane-aware policies on top.

### Pane Health Classification (from /system-performance-remediation)
| Age | CPU% | Status | Action |
|-----|------|--------|--------|
| <4h | >10% | Active | Protect |
| <4h | <2% | Thinking | Protect |
| 4-16h | >5% | Working | Check children |
| 4-16h | <2% | Possibly stuck | Flag for review |
| 16-24h | any | Likely stuck | Kill if only MCP children |
| >24h | any | Abandoned | Kill immediately |

### Components to build

1. **PaneHealthMonitor**: Background task sampling per-pane CPU%, memory, child count, age
   - Sample every 30s (configurable)
   - Store in ring buffer for trend analysis
   - Expose via MCP tool and CLI
   - **macOS**: Use libproc/proc_pidinfo instead of /proc/[pid]/stat

2. **PaneTriagePolicy**: Wraps process_triage TriageEngine with pane-aware context
   - Maps pane health classification to process_triage kill hierarchy
   - Adds FrankenTerm-specific categories: "agent thinking" vs "agent stuck"
   - Per-pane policy overrides via configuration

3. **AgeReaper**: Configurable age-based cleanup policies
   - Default: warn at 16h, kill at 24h
   - Per-pane overrides via configuration
   - Graceful shutdown: SIGTERM, wait 30s, SIGKILL

4. **Resource pressure response**: When PSI avg10 > 30% (Linux) or CPU load > 80% (macOS)
   - Renice oldest idle panes to nice=19, ionice class 3 (idle)
   - Pause non-essential captures
   - Warn user via notification

## Formal requirement
The stuck detector whack-a-mole logic lives in process_triage (wa-2zywl) with formal verification. This bead only needs to verify that PaneTriagePolicy correctly maps pane context to process_triage categories.

## Tests
- Simulate 200 panes with various ages/CPU profiles, verify correct classification
- Test PaneTriagePolicy maps to process_triage categories correctly
- Test age reaper respects graceful shutdown
- Load test: 200 pane health checks in <100ms total
- **Use criterion benchmarks for health check latency**

## Acceptance criteria
- Health classification matches the table above
- Delegates zombie/stuck/whack-a-mole to process_triage library
- Age reaper respects graceful shutdown
- CPU pressure triggers renice within 5s
- Works on both Linux (/proc) and macOS (libproc)
- All under /extreme-software-optimization methodology: profile before/after","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-10T16:10:51.270912Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-11T00:08:24.676026-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-11T00:08:24.676026-05:00","close_reason":"Closed","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-1ta8j","depends_on_id":"wa-2zywl","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T16:14:17.414441Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"wa-1ta8j","depends_on_id":"wa-a0owq","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T16:14:17.553827Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"}]}
 {"id":"wa-1u55z","title":"Replace TCP networking and TLS (tokio-rustls → asupersync)","description":"# Replace TCP networking and TLS

## Background
TCP and TLS are used by feature-gated modules: web server (web.rs), distributed mode (distributed.rs), and HTTP clients (reqwest).

## Scope

### TCP (web.rs)
- TcpListener for the HTTP API server
- Bound to configurable port, serves /health, /panes, /events, /search, etc.
- Currently uses fastapi::TcpServer wrapper around tokio TcpListener

### TLS (distributed.rs)
- tokio-rustls 0.26 for mTLS between distributed nodes
- Certificate and private key loading
- TLS 1.2+ configuration
- Client and server TLS config

### HTTP Client (reqwest)
- reqwest 0.12.12 with rustls-tls feature
- Used for outbound API calls
- Must be replaced with asupersync::http client

## Migration
```rust
// TCP Before
use tokio::net::TcpListener;
let listener = TcpListener::bind(addr).await?;

// TCP After
use asupersync::net::TcpListener;
let listener = TcpListener::bind(cx, addr).await?;

// TLS Before
use tokio_rustls::TlsConnector;

// TLS After
use asupersync::tls::TlsConnector;  // rustls-based, no tokio dep

// HTTP Before
let resp = reqwest::get(url).await?;

// HTTP After
let client = asupersync::http::Client::new();
let resp = client.get(cx, url).await?;
```

## Note on FastAPI/FastMCP
These vendored frameworks are built on tokio. They will need either:
1. Porting to asupersync (significant effort)
2. Replacing with asupersync-native HTTP/MCP server (if available)
3. Running in a compatibility shim (last resort)

This bead covers the direct TCP/TLS replacement. FastAPI/FastMCP refactoring is tracked separately.

## Acceptance criteria
- TCP server binds and accepts connections via asupersync
- TLS handshake works with asupersync TLS
- HTTP client makes successful outbound requests
- reqwest dependency removed
- tokio-rustls dependency removed

## macOS platform note
- **kqueue for TCP readiness**: On macOS, asupersync uses kqueue (not epoll) for TCP socket readiness notification (accept, read, write events). Verify that asupersync's reactor correctly uses kqueue for TcpListener and TcpStream on Darwin targets. If any platform-specific code exists for TCP handling, ensure `#[cfg(target_os = "macos")]` paths use kqueue and `#[cfg(target_os = "linux")]` paths use epoll. This is especially important for the web server's accept loop and the distributed module's TLS connections.

## Benchmark requirements
- **Criterion benchmarks for TLS handshake and throughput**: Add `benches/tcp_tls.rs` measuring:
  - TCP connection establishment latency (bind + connect + accept)
  - TLS handshake latency (client hello → finished) for TLS 1.2 and TLS 1.3
  - mTLS handshake latency (mutual certificate verification)
  - Sustained TCP throughput (MB/s) for streaming data over plain TCP
  - Sustained TLS throughput (MB/s) for streaming data over TLS
  - Comparison with tokio-rustls for equivalent TLS operations

## LabRuntime DPOR
- **Concurrent connection testing with DPOR**: Use LabRuntime schedule exploration (DPOR) to test concurrent TCP/TLS scenarios:
  - Multiple clients connecting to TcpListener simultaneously — verify all connections accepted, no drops
  - Interleaved TLS handshakes — verify no state corruption between concurrent handshakes
  - Race between connection accept and listener shutdown — verify clean teardown
  - Concurrent reads and writes on established TLS connections — verify no data corruption or reordering","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T03:49:05.178507Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T19:53:39.465924Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-1u55z","depends_on_id":"wa-2abzy","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T05:18:21.834643Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"wa-1u55z","depends_on_id":"wa-1yz79","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T05:18:24.65586Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"}]}

## Success Criteria
- Priority changes take effect immediately
- Overrides are visible via status/health output","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-01T03:05:00.210578659Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-11T01:36:32.039776-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-11T01:36:32.03978-05:00"}
+{"id":"wa-1u9qw","title":"Protocol-versioned local IPC namespace and compatibility handshake","description":"Goal: adopt contract-versioned local socket namespace and explicit compatibility handshake for FrankenTerm local IPC (robot and native). Why: prevent client/server skew from attaching to incompatible sessions. Scope: version local IPC endpoints by protocol or schema version, add handshake with explicit mismatch error payload (expected and actual version), define compatibility policy, and add integration tests for mismatch and upgrade. Deliverables: IPC versioning spec, endpoint discovery implementation changes, and compatibility tests with operator-visible diagnostics. Acceptance criteria: incompatible clients rejected deterministically with stable error code; compatible clients connect without behavior regressions; robot and native workflows keep stable semantics across patch upgrades. Cross-references: wa-vcjbi, wa-3kxe, wa-dr6zv, wa-3dfxb.13.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-12T06:44:08.350339Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-12T06:44:08.350339Z"}
 {"id":"wa-1uqi","title":"FTUI-03.2.a Route in-process logs through single output sink","description":"## Background
In-process output must obey one-writer constraints to keep rendering deterministic and avoid output corruption.

## Deliverables
- inventory and removal plan for direct stdout/stderr writes in UI-active paths
- unified output sink routing contract for logs/events/status lines
- verification checks to prove no bypass writes remain

## Acceptance Criteria
- all targeted paths route through the sanctioned sink while UI is active
- checks detect and fail on new bypass writes
- validation evidence includes unit assertions and runtime logs demonstrating routing correctness.,","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CalmLynx","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:14:05.996650629Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor","updated_at":"2026-02-09T05:12:15.499308732Z","closed_at":"2026-02-09T05:12:15.49917289Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-1uqi","depends_on_id":"wa-3cso","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:14:06.014840191Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor"},{"issue_id":"wa-1uqi","depends_on_id":"wa-3g47","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:24:35.653843104Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor"}]}
 {"id":"wa-1utb","title":"FTUI-02.1 Add ftui dependency stack and feature-gated integration path","description":"## Background\nwa must consume /dp/frankentui intentionally, not ad-hoc.\n\n## Deliverables\n- Cargo feature strategy for ftui adoption path\n- path/git pin policy to /dp/frankentui\n- explicit toggles for legacy/new tui runtime\n\n## Acceptance Criteria\n- project builds with and without ftui migration feature\n- dependency graph is documented and deterministic.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"LavenderGrove","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:07:39.031226995Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor","updated_at":"2026-02-09T01:18:40.996712962Z","closed_at":"2026-02-09T01:18:40.996571069Z","close_reason":"done","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-1utb","depends_on_id":"wa-1k52","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:07:39.046828394Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor"},{"issue_id":"wa-1utb","depends_on_id":"wa-136q","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:15:45.984694603Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor"}]}
 {"id":"wa-1wg","title":"[EPIC] Backup & Restore: Database Export, Import, Scheduled Backups","description":"# [EPIC] Backup & Restore System

## Mission
Provide users with confidence that their terminal history and configuration will never be lost, and make migration between machines or versions painless.

## Why This Matters
wa accumulates valuable data:
- Terminal transcripts (searchable history)
- Event timeline (debugging context)
- Configuration (tuned patterns, workflows)

Loss of this data would be a significant trust violation. Users need:
- Peace of mind that data is safe
- Easy migration to new machines
- Recovery from corruption or mistakes

## Components

### 1. Database Export (`wa backup export`)
Export the entire database to a portable format:
```bash
$ wa backup export --output backup_2026-01-18.wa
Creating backup...
  Segments: 12,345
  Events: 567
  Patterns: 23
  Config: included
Backup saved: backup_2026-01-18.wa (45 MB)
SHA256: abc123...
```

Export format:
- Self-describing archive (version header + manifest)
- SQLite dump + JSON metadata
- Optional compression (zstd)
- Integrity checksum

### 2. Database Import (`wa backup import`)
Restore from a backup:
```bash
$ wa backup import backup_2026-01-18.wa --verify
Verifying backup integrity... OK
Backup version: 1.2.0 (compatible)
Contents:
  Segments: 12,345
  Events: 567
  Config: included

Restore options:
  [1] Full restore (replace current data)
  [2] Merge (keep newer, import older)
  [3] Dry-run (show what would change)
```

Safety:
- Require explicit confirmation for full restore
- Create automatic backup before import
- Validate schema compatibility

### 3. Incremental Backups
For users who want continuous protection:
```toml
[backup]
enabled = true
schedule = "daily"  # or "hourly", "weekly"
retention_days = 30
destination = "~/.local/share/wa/backups/"
max_backups = 10
```

### 4. Configuration Export/Import
Separate from data, for sharing configs:
```bash
$ wa config export --output my-config.toml
$ wa config import my-config.toml --merge
```

### 5. Migration Assistant
When upgrading wa with breaking changes:
```bash
$ wa upgrade --from 1.x
Detected wa 1.x database
Migration required for:
  - Schema v1 → v2 (new event columns)
  - Config format update

Proceed? [y/N]
```

## Success Criteria
A user should be able to:
- create a backup file that is self-contained and verifiable (manifest + checksums)
- restore safely with a preview/dry-run path before any destructive change
- migrate between machines without manual file spelunking

Operational expectations (v0):
- backups are deterministic and explainable (stable manifest, clear versions)
- backups do not leak secrets (config redaction + no raw transcript dumping)
- imports refuse unsafe/incompatible inputs with actionable errors
- scheduled backups are robust (bounded disk usage, retention policy enforced)

## Testing
- Unit tests:
  - Export/import round-trip preserves all data
  - Version detection works across schema versions
  - Merge logic handles conflicts correctly

- Integration tests:
  - Backup with active watcher (snapshot isolation)
  - Import into empty vs populated database
  - Scheduled backup runs correctly

- E2E tests:
  - Full backup/restore cycle with real data
  - Migration from mock old version
  - Corrupt backup rejected with clear error

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] wa backup export creates portable backup files
- [ ] wa backup import restores with verification
- [ ] Incremental backup scheduling works
- [ ] wa config export/import for config-only migration
- [ ] Version migration assistant for upgrades
- [ ] All backups include integrity checksums
- [ ] Clear error messages for incompatible backups","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-01-18T19:55:22.915697374Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-29T02:58:36.963666064Z","closed_at":"2026-01-29T02:58:36.963516365Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-1wg","depends_on_id":"wa-37x","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:24Z","created_by":"import"}]}

 {"id":"wa-32z7","title":"Process re-launch engine — restart shells and agent processes in restored panes","description":"## Goal
Implement the process re-launch engine that restarts shells and AI agent processes in restored panes, using the process information captured in the MuxSnapshot.

## CRITICAL LIMITATION: Agent State is NOT Restored
**Re-launching an agent process (Claude Code, Codex, Gemini) starts a NEW session.** The agent in-memory state -- conversation history, context window, in-flight operations, tool results, working memory -- is permanently lost. Users MUST understand:
- Claude Code will start fresh with no knowledge of prior conversation
- Codex will lose its execution context and any pending operations
- Gemini will start a new session with default settings
- Any in-flight file edits, git operations, or multi-step tasks will be abandoned

This engine restores the PROCESS (correct binary, correct working directory, correct environment) but NOT the agent MIND. For true agent continuity, agents themselves must implement checkpoint/restore (out of scope for wa).

The primary value of process re-launch is:
1. **Shell restoration**: cd to correct working directory, restore shell environment
2. **Convenience**: User does not have to manually re-type launch commands for 50 panes
3. **Agent re-launch as opt-in shortcut**: Gets the agent running again, even if stateless

## Background and Motivation
After layout restoration and scrollback injection, panes have the right visual appearance but are running default shells (from the mux server spawn config). The original processes need to be re-launched.

This is the most complex restoration step because:
1. Not all processes CAN be restarted (some have ephemeral state)
2. Agent CLIs need specific arguments and environment variables
3. Working directories must be correct
4. Some processes need to be started in specific order (e.g., build watcher before agent)

## Technical Design

### Struct Definition
```rust
// Location: crates/wa-core/src/snapshot/restore_process.rs
pub struct ProcessLauncher {
    mux_client: Arc<DirectMuxClient>,
    config: LaunchConfig,
}

pub struct LaunchConfig {
    pub auto_launch: bool,         // default: false (require explicit opt-in)
    pub launch_shells: bool,       // default: true
    pub launch_agents: bool,       // default: false (safety: require explicit)
    pub launch_delay_ms: u64,      // default: 500 (stagger launches)
    pub agent_launch_commands: HashMap<String, String>,  // agent_type -> command template
}

pub struct ProcessPlan {
    pub pane_id: PaneId,
    pub original: ProcessInfo,
    pub action: LaunchAction,
    pub state_warning: Option<String>,  // Warning about state loss
}

pub enum LaunchAction {
    LaunchShell { shell: String, cwd: PathBuf },
    LaunchAgent { command: String, cwd: PathBuf, env: HashMap<String, String> },
    Skip { reason: String },
    Manual { hint: String },  // Tell user what was running
}
```

### Core Algorithm
1. **Plan phase** (dry-run safe, ALWAYS shown first):
   - For each pane in the snapshot, resolve the ProcessInfo
   - Determine LaunchAction based on process type and config
   - Shell processes -> LaunchShell
   - Known agent types -> LaunchAgent if opt-in enabled, else Manual with state loss warning
   - Unknown processes -> Manual with hint
   - Agent entries always include state_warning about context loss
   - Return ProcessPlan for review

2. **Execute phase** (requires explicit confirmation):
   - For each LaunchShell: send cd and exec shell to pane
   - For each LaunchAgent: send the configured launch command
   - Stagger launches by launch_delay_ms to prevent resource spikes
   - Track success/failure per pane

3. **Agent launch commands** (configurable in wa.toml):
```toml
[snapshots.agent_commands]
claude_code = "cd {cwd} && claude"
codex = "cd {cwd} && codex"
gemini = "cd {cwd} && gemini-cli"
```

### Plan Mode Output (wa snapshot restore --dry-run)
```
Pane  Original Process     Action         Warning
--------------------------------------------------------------
  0   bash @ /project      LaunchShell
  1   claude @ /project    Manual         Agent state will NOT be restored
  2   codex @ /other       Manual         Agent state will NOT be restored
  3   vim @ /project       Manual         Interactive program - manual restart needed
  4   bash @ /home         LaunchShell

Agents require --launch-agents flag. Agent processes start as NEW sessions
(conversation history, context, and in-flight work are lost).
```

### Safety Design
- By default, only shells are auto-launched (not agents)
- Agent re-launch requires explicit --launch-agents flag
- Plan mode is ALWAYS shown before execution (even with --launch-agents)
- Agent plans include prominent warning about state loss
- wa robot interface: plan returns JSON for programmatic review
- Each launch is logged with full command for audit trail

## Integration Points
- **PaneIdMap** from layout restoration: Maps to correct restored panes
- **ProcessInfo** from snapshot (bd-cuz schema): Original process details
- **AgentSessionRef** from snapshot: Links to wa agent session tracking
- **DirectMuxClient**: SendKeyStroke/SendPaste PDU for sending commands to panes
- **wa.toml config**: Agent launch command templates

## Key Files to Create/Modify
- CREATE: crates/wa-core/src/snapshot/restore_process.rs
- MODIFY: crates/wa-core/src/config.rs (add agent_commands config)
- MODIFY: crates/wa-core/src/snapshot/mod.rs

## Edge Cases
- Agent processes that need API keys in environment: use existing env or prompt
- Processes with file locks (e.g., cargo build): may fail to restart if lock exists
- Panes running interactive programs (vim, htop): skip with Manual hint
- Multiple agents in same working directory: stagger to avoid conflicts

## Dependencies
- bd-cuz: MuxSnapshot schema (ProcessInfo, AgentSessionRef)
- wa-e6pq: Layout restoration engine (provides PaneIdMap, panes must exist first)
- wa-1xcz: Scrollback injection (should complete before re-launch for visual continuity)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Shell processes re-launched in correct working directories
- Agent processes re-launched with correct commands (when opt-in enabled)
- Plan mode ALWAYS shows what would be launched, including state loss warnings
- Staggered launches prevent resource spikes
- Unknown processes reported as Manual with helpful hints
- Full audit trail of all launched commands
- Graceful handling of launch failures (continue with remaining panes)
- Agent state loss warning prominently displayed in plan output

## Estimated Effort
3-4 hours implementation, 1-2 hours testing

## macOS Compatibility
On macOS, the /proc/pid/cmdline filesystem does not exist. For recovering the original command line of a process during snapshot capture, use ps or sysctl instead:
```rust
#[cfg(target_os = "macos")]
fn get_process_cmdline(pid: i32) -> Result<Vec<String>> {
    // Use ps -o args= -p <pid> for command recovery
    let output = Command::new("ps")
        .args(&["-o", "args=", "-p", &pid.to_string()])
        .output()?;
    let cmdline = String::from_utf8_lossy(&output.stdout)
        .trim().to_string();
    Ok(shell_words::split(&cmdline)?)
}

#[cfg(target_os = "linux")]
fn get_process_cmdline(pid: i32) -> Result<Vec<String>> {
    let data = std::fs::read(format!("/proc/{}/cmdline", pid))?;
    Ok(data.split(|&b| b == 0)
        .filter(|s| !s.is_empty())
        .map(|s| String::from_utf8_lossy(s).to_string())
        .collect())
}
```
Note: ps -o args= may truncate long command lines. For full fidelity on macOS, also try sysctl kern.procargs2 via the sysctl crate as a fallback. Add shell_words to Cargo.toml for parsing the ps output.

## Test Requirements

### Criterion Benchmarks
Add benchmarks in crates/wa-core/benches/process_relaunch.rs:
- bench_plan_generation_50_panes: Generate ProcessPlan for 50 panes with mixed process types, target <10ms
- bench_shell_relaunch_latency: Measure time from launch command sent to shell prompt ready (single pane), target <500ms
- bench_staggered_launch_50_panes: Full staggered launch of 50 shell processes, measure wall-clock time vs. configured delay
- bench_process_info_resolution: Resolve ProcessInfo (command line, env, cwd) for 200 panes

### Property-Based Testing (proptest)
Add proptest cases in crates/wa-core/tests/proptest_process_relaunch.rs:
- **Process tree reconstruction**: For any arbitrary set of ProcessInfo entries (random shell types, random cwds, random agent types), the generated ProcessPlan must assign exactly one LaunchAction per pane, with no pane left unplanned. Strategy: arb_process_info_set() generates Vec of ProcessInfo with 1-100 entries.
- **Plan determinism**: For any ProcessInfo set and LaunchConfig, generating the plan twice must produce identical results (no non-determinism from HashMap iteration order, etc.).
- **Config-action consistency**: For any ProcessInfo, if launch_agents is false then no LaunchAgent actions may appear in the plan; if launch_shells is false then no LaunchShell actions may appear. Strategy: arb_launch_config() generates random boolean combinations.
- **Working directory validity**: For any LaunchShell or LaunchAgent action, the cwd must be a non-empty absolute path. Verify cwd.is_absolute() for all planned actions.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:32:46.886838Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T22:41:43.176747-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-10T22:41:43.176747-05:00","close_reason":"ProcessLauncher with plan/execute, LaunchConfig, agent detection, 21 tests.  {"id":"wa-35l","title":"wa robot workflow list: discover available workflows","description":"# wa robot workflow list

## Purpose
Allow agents to discover what workflows are available and their requirements.

## Command Interface
```bash
# List all workflows
wa robot workflow list

# Filter by trigger type
wa robot workflow list --trigger=event

# Include detailed descriptions
wa robot workflow list --verbose

# Filter by enabled status
wa robot workflow list --enabled

# JSON output (default in robot mode)
wa robot workflow list --json
```

## JSON Output Schema
```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "version": "0.1.0",
  "command": "workflow.list",
  "data": {
    "workflows": [
      {
        "name": "handle_compaction",
        "description": "Re-inject context after agent compaction",
        "enabled": true,
        "trigger_event_types": ["session.compaction"],
        "trigger_rule_ids": ["compaction.detected"],
        "agent_types": ["codex", "claude_code", "gemini"],
        "step_count": 5,
        "parameters": [],
        "requires_pane": true,
        "requires_approval": false,
        "can_abort": true,
        "destructive": false,
        "dependencies": []
      }
    ],
    "total": 5,
    "enabled_count": 4
  }
}
```

## Use Cases
1. **Discovery**: New agent learns what automations are available
2. **Capability check**: Agent verifies workflow exists before calling
3. **Documentation**: Generate help text from workflow metadata
4. **Debugging**: See what workflows could handle an event

## Implementation Notes
- Load workflow definitions from WorkflowRegistry
- Include both built-in and custom workflows
- Mark workflows with missing dependencies
- Filter by trigger type, enabled status

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests
```rust
#[test]
fn test_list_all_workflows() {
    let registry = setup_test_registry();
    let output = execute_robot(&["workflow", "list"]);
    
    assert!(output["ok"].as_bool().unwrap());
    let workflows = output["data"]["workflows"].as_array().unwrap();
    assert!(workflows.len() > 0);
    
    // Verify required fields present
    let wf = &workflows[0];
    assert!(wf["name"].is_string());
    assert!(wf["enabled"].is_boolean());
}

#[test]
fn test_list_filter_by_trigger() {
    let output = execute_robot(&["workflow", "list", "--trigger=event"]);
    
    let workflows = output["data"]["workflows"].as_array().unwrap();
    for wf in workflows {
        assert!(wf["trigger_event_types"].as_array().unwrap().len() > 0);
    }
}

#[test]
fn test_list_filter_enabled_only() {
    let output = execute_robot(&["workflow", "list", "--enabled"]);
    
    let workflows = output["data"]["workflows"].as_array().unwrap();
    for wf in workflows {
        assert!(wf["enabled"].as_bool().unwrap());
    }
}

#[test]
fn test_list_json_schema_validation() {
    let output = execute_robot(&["workflow", "list"]);
    validate_against_schema(&output, "wa-robot-workflow-list.json");
}

#[test]
fn test_list_verbose_includes_descriptions() {
    let output = execute_robot(&["workflow", "list", "--verbose"]);
    
    let workflows = output["data"]["workflows"].as_array().unwrap();
    for wf in workflows {
        assert!(wf["description"].is_string());
        assert!(!wf["description"].as_str().unwrap().is_empty());
    }
}
```

### E2E Test
```bash
#!/bin/bash
# e2e_workflow_list.sh
set -euo pipefail
LOG="${ARTIFACT_DIR:-/tmp}/workflow_list.log"
log() { echo "[$(date -Iseconds)] $*" | tee -a "$LOG"; }

log "=== Workflow List E2E ==="

# 1. Basic list
log "Testing basic list..."
LIST=$(wa robot workflow list)
log "Result: $LIST"
echo "$LIST" | jq -e '.ok == true' || { log "FAIL: ok not true"; exit 1; }
echo "$LIST" | jq -e '.data.workflows | length > 0' || { log "FAIL: no workflows"; exit 1; }

# 2. Filter by trigger
log "Testing trigger filter..."
FILTERED=$(wa robot workflow list --trigger=event)
log "Filtered: $FILTERED"
echo "$FILTERED" | jq -e '.ok == true' || { log "FAIL: filter failed"; exit 1; }

# 3. Verify counts
log "Verifying counts..."
TOTAL=$(echo "$LIST" | jq '.data.total')
ENABLED=$(echo "$LIST" | jq '.data.enabled_count')
log "Total: $TOTAL, Enabled: $ENABLED"
[ "$ENABLED" -le "$TOTAL" ] || { log "FAIL: enabled > total"; exit 1; }

log "=== PASS: workflow_list ==="
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Lists all registered workflows
- [ ] Includes trigger and agent type info
- [ ] Shows dependency requirements
- [ ] --trigger filter works
- [ ] --enabled filter works
- [ ] --verbose includes full descriptions
- [ ] JSON validates against wa-robot-workflow-list.json schema
- [ ] Unit tests pass
- [ ] E2E test passes with detailed logging

## Cross-reference
See **bd-qvbz** for comprehensive integration tests covering the full workflow lifecycle.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-18T19:12:48.141113151Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-30T04:45:41.506988096Z","closed_at":"2026-01-30T04:45:41.506917374Z","close_reason":"done","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-35l","depends_on_id":"wa-2vr1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:24Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-35l","depends_on_id":"wa-mxzp","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:24Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-35l","depends_on_id":"wa-4vx.7.10","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:24Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-35l","depends_on_id":"wa-7dd","type":"relates-to","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:24Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-35l","depends_on_id":"wa-nu4.1.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:24Z","created_by":"import"}]}

 {"id":"wa-36idf","title":"Refactor generic Pool (pool.rs) to asupersync primitives","description":"# Refactor generic Pool (pool.rs) to asupersync primitives

## Background
pool.rs (554 LOC) is the generic async connection pool used by MuxPool. It's the most Semaphore-heavy module and a critical performance path.

## Current implementation uses
- tokio::sync::Semaphore for concurrency limiting
- tokio::sync::OwnedSemaphorePermit for permit management
- tokio::sync::Mutex<VecDeque<>> for idle connection storage
- Atomic counters for statistics
- tokio::time::timeout for acquire timeouts

## Migration plan
1. Replace Semaphore with asupersync::sync::Semaphore (permit obligations)
2. Replace Mutex<VecDeque<>> with asupersync::sync::Mutex
3. Replace timeout with Budget::deadline
4. Thread Cx through all async methods: acquire(), try_acquire(), put(), evict_idle(), clear()

## Critical: Pool semantics must be preserved
- Fairness: FIFO acquire ordering
- Idle eviction: connections older than max_idle_time are removed
- Stats: active_count, idle_count, total_created, total_destroyed, waits, timeouts
- Health checks: validate connections before returning from acquire()
- Concurrency limit: max_size connections at any time

## Comprehensive unit tests (LabRuntime, seed-locked)
1. **Basic acquire/release**: acquire connection, verify stats, release, verify stats
2. **Concurrent acquire**: N tasks acquire simultaneously, verify max_size respected
3. **Acquire timeout**: pool full, acquire with Budget::deadline, verify Cancelled outcome
4. **Idle eviction**: put connection, advance virtual time past idle timeout, verify evicted
5. **Health check on acquire**: mock unhealthy connection, verify pool creates new one
6. **Stats accuracy**: exercise all stats counters, verify at each step
7. **Permit obligation**: verify Semaphore permit is properly tracked by obligation system
8. **Stress test**: 100 concurrent acquire/release cycles, no panics, no leaks
9. **Cancellation safety**: cancel mid-acquire, verify no leaked permits or connections

Each test should log: acquire attempts, permit grants, connection creates/destroys, eviction events, stats snapshots.

## Acceptance criteria
- Pool works identically with asupersync primitives
- Semaphore permit obligations properly tracked
- Acquire timeout works via Budget
- Idle eviction timer works with virtual time
- Pool statistics accurate under concurrency
- 9+ LabRuntime tests, all seed-locked and deterministic
- Obligation leak oracle passes for all tests

## LabRuntime DPOR
- **Concurrent pool access testing with DPOR**: Use LabRuntime schedule exploration (DPOR) to exhaustively test concurrent pool operations:
  - Multiple tasks acquiring/releasing simultaneously — verify no double-checkout, no permit leak
  - Interleaved acquire + eviction — verify eviction doesn't corrupt active connections
  - Concurrent health checks — verify no race between health check failure and new connection creation
  - Cancellation during acquire while another task releases — verify permit is correctly recycled

## Benchmark requirements
- **Criterion benchmarks for pool checkout/return latency**: Add `benches/pool_ops.rs` measuring:
  - Uncontended acquire + release cycle latency
  - Contended acquire latency with N=2,4,8,16 concurrent tasks on pool of size 4
  - Idle eviction scan cost as a function of idle connection count
  - Stats counter update overhead (atomic operations)
  - Comparison with tokio-based pool implementation for equivalent operations

## Concurrency verification
- **Loom for lock-free pool internals**: Use Loom to model-check the atomic statistics counters and any lock-free fast paths in the pool implementation. Specifically verify that atomic counter updates (active_count, idle_count) remain consistent under all interleavings and that no ABA problems exist in the permit lifecycle.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T03:50:07.941528Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T19:49:08.786488Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-36idf","depends_on_id":"wa-3d14m","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T03:51:58.542135Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"wa-36idf","depends_on_id":"wa-1yz79","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T03:51:58.656529Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"}]}

 {"id":"wa-3iax","title":"IPC config + socket lifecycle design","description":"## What
Define IPC config options and socket lifecycle management.

## Why
We need safe defaults and predictable file paths for local clients.

## How
- Config: enabled flag, socket path, permissions
- Lifecycle: create/remove socket, handle stale sockets

## Success Criteria
- Socket path is deterministic and safe

Why: Zellij watcher and per-client state model maps directly to FrankenTerm orchestration and reduces ambiguity around shared focus. Scope: define client roles (interactive vs watcher read-only); track per-client view state (active tab, active pane, mirrored or independent view); enforce role-based policy for mutating actions; expose role/view info in robot state and session APIs.  {"id":"wa-3k2g1","title":"Make luahelper crate fully optional behind lua feature","description":"# Make luahelper Crate Fully Optional

## Context (revised from "Remove luahelper dependency")
Under the dual-runtime strategy, luahelper stays in the tree but becomes optional. When the `lua` feature is disabled, no crate should depend on luahelper.

## Crates that currently depend on luahelper
1. config — for Lua config parsing → gate behind `lua` feature
2. mux — for Lua domain management → gate behind `lua` feature
3. procinfo — optional, already has lua feature → verify it's gated
4. termwiz-funcs — for Lua termwiz bindings → gate behind `lua` feature

## What to do
1. Verify each crate's luahelper dependency is behind `lua` feature flag
2. Ensure luahelper itself only compiles when at least one dependent enables `lua`
3. Add workspace-level feature: `workspace-lua = ["config/lua", "mux/lua", ...]`
4. Test: with no-lua, luahelper should not appear in `cargo tree` output

## Success criteria
- `cargo check --no-default-features` compiles without luahelper in dep tree
- `cargo check` (default, with lua) still includes luahelper and works
- `cargo tree -p config --no-default-features | grep luahelper` returns nothing
- All existing Lua tests pass when lua feature is enabled

## Why not remove entirely
luahelper contains useful Rust↔Lua bridging code. Users who want Lua scripting (backward compat with wezterm.lua) still need it. Removing it would break the Lua path.

## Test Framework Requirements
- **Proptest**: Property-based tests for feature flag combinations:
  - Generate random subsets of feature flags from {lua, wasm, no-lua, frankenterm, vendored} and verify that cargo check succeeds for all valid combinations
  - Verify that incompatible combinations (e.g., lua + no-lua simultaneously) are rejected at compile time or produce clear errors
  - For each valid feature combination, verify luahelper appears in `cargo tree` if and only if `lua` is enabled

## Cross-References
- **wa-ggw9w** (Config crate Lua optional): The config crate's `lua` feature flag must be coordinated with luahelper's optionality. When config disables `lua`, its luahelper dep must disappear.
- **wa-2ufom** (Mux crate Lua optional): Same coordination needed for the mux crate — its luahelper dep must be gated behind `lua`.
- Together, wa-3k2g1 + wa-ggw9w + wa-2ufom form the complete "Lua-optional" triad. All three must be implemented together for a clean no-Lua build.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T06:52:48.067258Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T19:43:45.587886Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-3k2g1","depends_on_id":"wa-ggw9w","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T06:52:56.890086Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"wa-3k2g1","depends_on_id":"wa-2ufom","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T06:52:56.999672Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"}]}

We need explicit adapters that isolate rendering from backend schema evolution.\n\n## Deliverables\n- mapping modules for pane/event/triage/history/search/workflow views\n- normalization and redaction-safe formatting rules\n- unit tests on adapter transformations\n\n## Acceptance Criteria\n- adapters produce deterministic, render-ready view models\n- schema evolution does not require direct widget rewrites.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"PinkMountain","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:08:00.269912667Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor","updated_at":"2026-02-09T01:25:54.877233043Z","closed_at":"2026-02-09T01:25:54.877099585Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-3kal","depends_on_id":"wa-1ssn","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:08:00.285785871Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor"},{"issue_id":"wa-3kal","depends_on_id":"wa-5htt","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:16:49.969223411Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor"},{"issue_id":"wa-3kal","depends_on_id":"wa-8q4e","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:16:54.789966822Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor"}]}
 {"id":"wa-3kxe","title":"[EPIC] Fork Hardening — Fix WezTerm mux server root causes","description":"## Goal
Fix root causes in the forked WezTerm mux server that cause it to die under agent swarm load, moving beyond client-side workarounds to true reliability.

## Background & Motivation
From the user's X post: "I'm just so tired of WezTerm dying horribly under the extreme load of my agent swarm sessions and losing time and work. It just wasn't built for these assumptions."

The wa-3cyp performance epic focuses on CLIENT-side optimizations (polling, pooling, compression). This epic goes INTO THE WEZTERM CODE ITSELF to fix the root causes:
1. Memory leaks in the mux server (76GB RSS after 23 days observed)
2. No memory limits or OOM prevention
3. No incremental snapshot capability
4. Lock contention in hot paths

The user decided "I want to rename it to FrankenTerm and control the entire stack all the way for full control." This epic delivers on that promise by making the fork genuinely more reliable than upstream WezTerm for agent swarm workloads.

## Design Philosophy (Extreme Software Optimization)
Every change follows the ESO loop:
1. BASELINE — Measure current behavior (hyperfine, heaptrack)
2. PROFILE — Identify actual hotspots (not guesses)
3. PROVE — Golden outputs + isomorphism proof per change
4. IMPLEMENT — Only changes with Opportunity Score ≥ 2.0
5. VERIFY — sha256sum -c golden_checksums.txt
6. REPEAT — Re-profile (bottlenecks shift)

## Scope
This epic covers 5 targeted changes to the forked WezTerm mux server:
1. Memory leak root cause analysis and patches
2. cgroups memory budget and OOM prevention
3. Differential snapshots for continuous saving
4. Lock-free capture pipeline
5. Operational telemetry pipeline

## Relationship to Existing Beads
- Extends wa-3cyp (performance) — but focuses on WezTerm server code, not wa client
- Enables wa-rsaf (session persistence) — differential snapshots enable continuous saving
- Complements wa-2cha (watchdog) — OOM prevention adds another layer of protection

## Dependencies
- FrankenTerm rename (wa-vv3h) should ideally complete first (we're modifying forked code)
- No hard blockers — can start profiling immediately

## Acceptance Criteria
- Memory leak root causes identified and patched with isomorphism proofs
- cgroups memory budget prevents OOM with proactive save triggers
- Differential snapshots reduce save time to sub-100ms
- Lock-free capture eliminates contention in hot path
- Telemetry pipeline provides full observability
- All changes follow ESO loop (profile → prove → implement → verify)
- Comprehensive benchmark suite for regression detection","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:23:46.320024Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-09T21:23:46.320024Z"}
